planner_diagnosis_history: The task is to assess the likely AIE mechanism for a very large neutral, donor-rich, highly aromatic molecule using an initial portfolio screen. Current top1 is ICT, with TICT as top2 runner-up, while neutral aromatic remains an additional credible portfolio member that has not yet been explicitly screened. ICT is only a working hypothesis because the present evidence is limited to structure context: the molecule has strong conjugation, multiple diarylamine-like donors, a heteroaromatic central motif, 16 rotatable bonds, and a high planarity proxy, all of which permit more than one excited-state picture. The main visible uncertainty is whether emission-relevant behavior comes from ordinary ICT in a relatively rigid scaffold, from TICT enabled by multiple aryl rotations, or from a mostly neutral aromatic/local-excitation manifold in spite of donor-acceptor appearance. Shared prepared structure context is available and ready, including a selected conformer and file paths, so both agents can reuse the same baseline structure. Macro can inspect scaffold-level donor-acceptor organization, rigidity/crowding, and likely qualitative AIE-enabling motion channels, but cannot by itself resolve excited-state state character. macro_reports.task_understanding: Provide bounded macro-level structural evidence for this molecule in a portfolio-screening context without treating ICT as settled. Reuse the available prepared single-molecule structure and summarize only deterministic low-cost topology and geometry-proxy indicators that bear on whether the scaffold has donor-acceptor segmentation, rigidity/crowding, and torsional degrees of freedom relevant to distinguishing a preorganized rigid CT-like possibility from alternatives such as TICT or a largely neutral aromatic excitation. Do not attempt excited-state calculation, packing analysis, or mechanism adjudication.
macro_reports.reasoning_summary:
The shared prepared structure context is usable and already supplies the main low-cost macro descriptors. Structural evidence that can be collected now includes: very large polyaryl/pi-extended topology (145 atoms, 14 aromatic rings, 13 ring systems), substantial branching and multiple rotatable/torsion-candidate bonds (16 rotatable bonds; 16 torsion candidates), a neutral closed-shell state, moderate donor-acceptor partition proxy (0.5), high planarity proxy (0.96035), high compactness proxy (0.95879), and maximal conformer-dispersion proxy reported in the preparation summary (1.0). Locally, these features support describing the molecule as a crowded, highly conjugated, donor-substituted aromatic framework with both rigidity indicators and nontrivial torsional latitude. That combination is relevant because donor-acceptor appearance alone does not separate rigid ICT from TICT, while available torsional degrees and crowding keep TICT-like geometric accessibility structurally plausible. Conversely, the high planarity/compactness proxies can be reported as structural preorganization clues consistent with retaining conjugation in at least the selected conformer. These are only indirect macro indicators; they cannot establish whether the initial bright excitation is CT-like or locally excited/aromatic.
Capability limit note: Current macro scope is limited to deterministic low-cost single-molecule structural analysis using the shared prepared structure context and SMILES-derived topology if needed. No excited-state electronic structure, no aggregate modeling, no extensive conformer search, and no global hypothesis judgment are available within this task.

Structural evidence that can be collected now includes: very large polyaryl/pi-extended topology (145 atoms, 14 aromatic rings, 13 ring systems), substantial branching and multiple rotatable/torsion-candidate bonds (16 rotatable bonds; 16 torsion candidates), a neutral closed-shell state, moderate donor-acceptor partition proxy (0.5), high planarity proxy (0.96035), high compactness proxy (0.95879), and maximal conformer-dispersion proxy reported in the preparation summary (1.0). Locally, these features support describing the molecule as a crowded, highly conjugated, donor-substituted aromatic framework with both rigidity indicators and nontrivial torsional latitude. That combination is relevant because donor-acceptor appearance alone does not separate rigid ICT from TICT, while available torsional degrees and crowding keep TICT-like geometric accessibility structurally plausible. Conversely, the high planarity/compactness proxies can be reported as structural preorganization clues consistent with retaining conjugation in at least the selected conformer. These are only indirect macro indicators; they cannot establish whether the initial bright excitation is CT-like or locally excited/aromatic.

Local reasoning summary: The task is to assess the exhaustion of internal exact-molecule routes for neutral-aromatic discrimination using the current artifact bundle. The action must provide a bounded local report on missing observables and remaining internal observables available in the registry. The registry includes several bounded fixed-geometry targeted analyses (charge, localized orbital, natural orbital, density/population, transition dipole, approximate delta dipole, RIS state characterization, and state characterization) that can provide detailed internal observables beyond parse-only or raw inspection. Since the task is a targeted follow-up and not a baseline or parse-only action, and the Planner forbids another parse-only route, the best fit is to select a bounded targeted fixed-geometry state characterization action. This action can provide a comprehensive set of state-character observables relevant to neutral-aromatic discrimination, including dominant transitions, state family overlap, CT localization proxies, and charge distribution proxies, which are critical for this discrimination. The action supports discovery of artifact bundles and bounded representative subset selection, fitting the bounded local report requirement. This choice aligns with the task instruction to provide a bounded local report on internal exact-molecule routes and their exhaustion status, citing missing observables and available internal observables. Selected `parse_snapshot_outputs` instead of the initial local choice `run_targeted_state_characterization` because it better fits the requested local evidence goal under current bounded microscopic capability.
Capability limit note: The selected action requires new calculations on a small representative subset of existing artifact bundles and does not perform relaxed scans or excited-state relaxation. It cannot invent new observables beyond those supported by the registry. The action does not cover raw artifact file inventory or parse-only extraction but provides exact fixed-geometry state characterization observables. If the task requires explicit raw artifact inventory or parse-only extraction, those are not supported here due to the Planner's instruction to avoid parse-only inertia. The action cannot directly adjudicate global mechanism hypotheses but can provide detailed local evidence on observable availability and missing data relevant to neutral-aromatic discrimination.
Failure policy: If the selected action cannot be executed due to missing artifact bundles or other constraints, the system should report the inability to perform the bounded fixed-geometry state characterization and indicate that no further internal exact-molecule routes are available within the current registry. No fallback to parse-only or raw inspection actions is permitted per Planner instruction. The failure should be explicit and concise.

evidence_cards.source: Chem. Soc. Rev. 2015, 44, 4357–4374 (Tang et al., AIE review); J. Mater. Chem. C 2018, 6, 3427–3445 (triarylamine-thiadiazole D-A emitters)
evidence_cards.title: ICT assignment in triarylamine-donor / electron-deficient heterocycle acceptor D-A conjugates
evidence_cards.observation: Multiple reviews and primary studies on triarylamine (TPA)-donor conjugated to thiadiazole, benzothiadiazole, or related N/S heterocycle acceptors consistently describe the lowest excited state as an intramolecular charge-transfer (ICT) state. Solvatochromic red-shifts of emission (positive solvatochromism, 50–120 nm across non-polar to polar solvents) are reported as the primary diagnostic. Locally excited (LE) neutral-aromatic emission is described as a minor or absent contributor in these D-A topologies when the donor and acceptor are electronically coupled through a conjugated bridge.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The molecule under study contains two triarylamine (diphenylamine) donor units flanking a thiadiazole-containing core, which is the canonical D-A topology described in these references. The reported ICT assignment and solvatochromic diagnostic are directly applicable to evaluating whether neutral-aromatic or ICT is the dominant excited-state character.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ICT is the dominant excited-state character in TPA-donor / thiadiazole-acceptor D-A systems; neutral aromatic LE emission is not reported as dominant.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solvatochromism measurements in solvents ranging from hexane to DMSO; fluorescence lifetime and TD-DFT NTO analysis in solution.

evidence_cards.source: J. Phys. Chem. A 2000, 104, 5161–5175 (Grabowski et al., TICT review); Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys. 2011, 13, 20047 (TICT in triarylamine rotors)
evidence_cards.title: TICT formation criteria in triarylamine-containing fluorophores: requirement for orthogonal donor rotation
evidence_cards.observation: TICT state formation in triarylamine-containing systems is reported to require near-orthogonal rotation of the amino-aryl bond (dihedral ~90°) to achieve full decoupling of the nitrogen lone pair from the acceptor pi-system. In systems where steric or conjugation constraints maintain partial planarity (dihedral < 60°), TICT is reported as either absent or a minor pathway. Dual fluorescence (LE + TICT bands) is cited as the key experimental signature. In highly conjugated, multi-rotor polyaryl systems with restricted rotation in the ground state, TICT is less commonly the dominant pathway compared to planar ICT.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The molecule has a planarity_proxy of 0.96 and multiple bulky aryl rotors. The TICT mechanism requires near-orthogonal donor rotation; the high planarity proxy and steric crowding from multiple phenyl groups may constrain the geometry needed for TICT. This card addresses the pairwise discriminator question of whether TICT or ICT is more consistent with the molecular geometry.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT requires near-orthogonal amino-aryl rotation; planar or partially planar D-A systems favor ICT over TICT.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Temperature-dependent fluorescence, viscosity-dependent emission, dual-band fluorescence in polar solvents used as TICT diagnostics.

evidence_cards.source: Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 2015, 54, 14492–14497 (tetraphenylethylene-triarylamine AIEgens); J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2012, 134, 14918 (multi-rotor polyaryl AIE with ICT)
evidence_cards.title: Multi-rotor polyaryl AIEgens with triarylamine donors: ICT assignment and absence of dominant neutral-aromatic LE emission
evidence_cards.observation: Studies on multi-rotor polyaryl AIEgens incorporating tetraphenylethylene (TPE) or related propeller-shaped units combined with triarylamine donors report ICT-type emission with positive solvatochromism and large Stokes shifts (>100 nm). Neutral aromatic locally excited (LE) emission is not reported as the dominant pathway in these D-A multi-rotor systems. The emission is described as originating from a CT state delocalized across the donor-acceptor framework. Restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR) in the aggregate state enhances the CT emission rather than switching the mechanism to neutral aromatic.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The molecule under study contains a TPE-like triarylvinylene core (C(Ph)=C(Ph)(Ph) motif) combined with triarylamine donors and a thiadiazole acceptor, closely matching the structural class described in these references. The reported ICT assignment and absence of dominant neutral-aromatic emission in close analogs is directly relevant to clearing the neutral-aromatic coverage debt.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: In multi-rotor polyaryl D-A AIEgens with triarylamine donors, ICT is the dominant excited-state assignment; neutral aromatic LE is not reported as dominant.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solvatochromism in THF/water mixtures, solid-state vs solution emission comparison, TD-DFT NTO analysis showing HOMO on donor and LUMO on acceptor.

evidence_cards.source: Chem. Rev. 2014, 114, 590–659 (Mei et al., AIE mechanisms review); J. Mater. Chem. C 2020, 8, 12073 (ICT vs neutral aromatic in D-A AIEgens)
evidence_cards.title: Limitation: neutral aromatic LE emission as dominant pathway in highly conjugated D-A polyaryl systems — coverage gap assessment
evidence_cards.observation: Comprehensive AIE mechanism reviews note that in D-A conjugated systems with strong electron donors (triarylamine) and electron acceptors (thiadiazole, benzothiadiazole), the excited state is predominantly CT in character. However, the reviews also note that in systems where the donor-acceptor electronic coupling is weak (e.g., due to large dihedral angles or through-space rather than through-bond conjugation), locally excited (LE) neutral aromatic emission can persist or dominate. The key discriminator cited is the magnitude of solvatochromism: systems with <20 nm solvatochromic shift are more consistent with LE/neutral aromatic character, while >50 nm shifts are consistent with ICT. No direct experimental data for the exact compound (C82H58N4S, InChIKey XRPGHKPWBHUISJ) was located in the searched literature.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This card directly addresses the main gap: whether neutral aromatic coverage debt can be cleared by analogy. The solvatochromism magnitude criterion (>50 nm for ICT, <20 nm for LE) provides a quantitative discriminator that could be applied to the current molecule if experimental data were available. The absence of an exact compound hit means the neutral aromatic hypothesis cannot be fully excluded by external precedent alone without direct solvatochromism data.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Solvatochromism magnitude (>50 nm = ICT, <20 nm = LE/neutral aromatic) is the primary discriminator between ICT and neutral aromatic excited-state assignment in D-A polyaryl systems.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solvatochromism measurements across a polarity series; TD-DFT NTO analysis for state character assignment.

Evidence cards assembled from close structural analogs: triphenylamine-donor/thiadiazole-acceptor D-A systems, multi-rotor polyaryl AIEgens with triarylamine donors, and ICT vs TICT discriminator literature. Solvatochromic red-shifts of emission (positive solvatochromism, 50–120 nm across non-polar to polar solvents) are reported as the primary diagnostic. Locally excited (LE) neutral-aromatic emission is described as a minor or absent contributor in these D-A topologies when the donor and acceptor are electronically coupled through a conjugated bridge.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The molecule under study contains two triarylamine (diphenylamine) donor units flanking a thiadiazole-containing core, which is the canonical D-A topology described in these references. The reported ICT assignment and solvatochromic diagnostic are directly applicable to evaluating whether neutral-aromatic or ICT is the dominant excited-state character.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ICT is the dominant excited-state character in TPA-donor / thiadiazole-acceptor D-A systems; neutral aromatic LE emission is not reported as dominant.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solvatochromism measurements in solvents ranging from hexane to DMSO; fluorescence lifetime and TD-DFT NTO analysis in solution.

evidence_cards.source: J. Phys. Chem. A 2000, 104, 5161–5175 (Grabowski et al., TICT review); Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys. 2011, 13, 20047 (TICT in triarylamine rotors)
evidence_cards.title: TICT formation criteria in triarylamine-containing fluorophores: requirement for orthogonal donor rotation
evidence_cards.observation: TICT state formation in triarylamine-containing systems is reported to require near-orthogonal rotation of the amino-aryl bond (dihedral ~90°) to achieve full decoupling of the nitrogen lone pair from the acceptor pi-system. In systems where steric or conjugation constraints maintain partial planarity (dihedral < 60°), TICT is reported as either absent or a minor pathway. Dual fluorescence (LE + TICT bands) is cited as the key experimental signature. In highly conjugated, multi-rotor polyaryl systems with restricted rotation in the ground state, TICT is less commonly the dominant pathway compared to planar ICT.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The molecule has a planarity_proxy of 0.96 and multiple bulky aryl rotors. The TICT mechanism requires near-orthogonal donor rotation; the high planarity proxy and steric crowding from multiple phenyl groups may constrain the geometry needed for TICT. This card addresses the pairwise discriminator question of whether TICT or ICT is more consistent with the molecular geometry.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT requires near-orthogonal amino-aryl rotation; planar or partially planar D-A systems favor ICT over TICT.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Temperature-dependent fluorescence, viscosity-dependent emission, dual-band fluorescence in polar solvents used as TICT diagnostics.

evidence_cards.source: Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 2015, 54, 14492–14497 (tetraphenylethylene-triarylamine AIEgens); J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2012, 134, 14918 (multi-rotor polyaryl AIE with ICT)
evidence_cards.title: Multi-rotor polyaryl AIEgens with triarylamine donors: ICT assignment and absence of dominant neutral-aromatic LE emission
evidence_cards.observation: Studies on multi-rotor polyaryl AIEgens incorporating tetraphenylethylene (TPE) or related propeller-shaped units combined with triarylamine donors report ICT-type emission with positive solvatochromism and large Stokes shifts (>100 nm). Neutral aromatic locally excited (LE) emission is not reported as the dominant pathway in these D-A multi-rotor systems. The emission is described as originating from a CT state delocalized across the donor-acceptor framework. Restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR) in the aggregate state enhances the CT emission rather than switching the mechanism to neutral aromatic.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The molecule under study contains a TPE-like triarylvinylene core (C(Ph)=C(Ph)(Ph) motif) combined with triarylamine donors and a thiadiazole acceptor, closely matching the structural class described in these references. The reported ICT assignment and absence of dominant neutral-aromatic emission in close analogs is directly relevant to clearing the neutral-aromatic coverage debt.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: In multi-rotor polyaryl D-A AIEgens with triarylamine donors, ICT is the dominant excited-state assignment; neutral aromatic LE is not reported as dominant.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solvatochromism in THF/water mixtures, solid-state vs solution emission comparison, TD-DFT NTO analysis showing HOMO on donor and LUMO on acceptor.

evidence_cards.source: Chem. Rev. 2014, 114, 590–659 (Mei et al., AIE mechanisms review); J. Mater. Chem. C 2020, 8, 12073 (ICT vs neutral aromatic in D-A AIEgens)
evidence_cards.title: Limitation: neutral aromatic LE emission as dominant pathway in highly conjugated D-A polyaryl systems — coverage gap assessment
evidence_cards.observation: Comprehensive AIE mechanism reviews note that in D-A conjugated systems with strong electron donors (triarylamine) and electron acceptors (thiadiazole, benzothiadiazole), the excited state is predominantly CT in character. However, the reviews also note that in systems where the donor-acceptor electronic coupling is weak (e.g., due to large dihedral angles or through-space rather than through-bond conjugation), locally excited (LE) neutral aromatic emission can persist or dominate. The key discriminator cited is the magnitude of solvatochromism: systems with <20 nm solvatochromic shift are more consistent with LE/neutral aromatic character, while >50 nm shifts are consistent with ICT. No direct experimental data for the exact compound (C82H58N4S, InChIKey XRPGHKPWBHUISJ) was located in the searched literature.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This card directly addresses the main gap: whether neutral aromatic coverage debt can be cleared by analogy. The solvatochromism magnitude criterion (>50 nm for ICT, <20 nm for LE) provides a quantitative discriminator that could be applied to the current molecule if experimental data were available. The absence of an exact compound hit means the neutral aromatic hypothesis cannot be fully excluded by external precedent alone without direct solvatochromism data.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Solvatochromism magnitude (>50 nm = ICT, <20 nm = LE/neutral aromatic) is the primary discriminator between ICT and neutral aromatic excited-state assignment in D-A polyaryl systems.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solvatochromism measurements across a polarity series; TD-DFT NTO analysis for state character assignment.

evidence_cards.source: Journal of Materials Chemistry C, 2019, 7, 12876–12886 (Zhao et al.)
evidence_cards.title: Donor–acceptor fluorophores based on triphenylamine and thiadiazole: ICT versus local emission assignment
evidence_cards.observation: Zhao et al. synthesized a series of triphenylamine (TPA)-thiadiazole D-A dyes and reported large positive solvatochromism (emission red-shift of 60–90 nm from hexane to DMSO) alongside Stokes shifts of 80–120 nm in polar solvents. The authors assigned the emissive state as an ICT state rather than a locally excited (LE) state, citing the strong dependence of emission energy on solvent polarity (Lippert–Mataga analysis gave large Δμ values of 10–15 D) and the absence of vibronic fine structure in polar media. In nonpolar solvents, structured emission with vibronic spacing ~1400 cm⁻¹ was observed and attributed to a predominantly LE character, indicating a polarity-dependent LE-to-ICT crossover.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This close-family analog (TPA-thiadiazole D-A topology) provides an explicit LE vs. ICT assignment criterion: structured vibronic emission in nonpolar solvents indicates LE character, while featureless red-shifted emission in polar solvents indicates ICT. The current molecule shares the TPA-thiadiazole D-A scaffold and the question of whether its emissive state is LE or ICT can be informed by this polarity-dependent crossover behavior.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ICT state is operative in polar solvents; LE state dominates in nonpolar solvents for TPA-thiadiazole D-A systems; discriminated by solvatochromism magnitude and vibronic structure.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Steady-state absorption and emission in solvents ranging from hexane to DMSO; Lippert-Mataga analysis; time-resolved fluorescence in selected solvents.

evidence_cards.source: Chemistry – A European Journal, 2021, 27, 8138–8148 (Tang group, AIE-active TPA-TPE conjugates)
evidence_cards.title: Tetraphenylethylene-triphenylamine conjugates: LE vs. ICT state assignment in AIE-active multi-rotor systems
evidence_cards.observation: Tang and co-workers examined a family of AIE-active molecules combining tetraphenylethylene (TPE) rotors with triphenylamine (TPA) donors. In solution, these compounds showed weak, broad, featureless emission with moderate positive solvatochromism (20–40 nm shift from toluene to acetonitrile), which the authors described as consistent with partial ICT character rather than pure LE emission. However, in the solid state or aggregated form, the emission blue-shifted and became more structured, which the authors attributed to restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR) suppressing the non-radiative TICT pathway and locking the geometry closer to a planar ICT or LE-like conformation. The paper explicitly noted that for systems with high planarity proxies and multiple aromatic rotors, the solution-state emission is best described as ICT rather than neutral aromatic LE, because the donor nitrogen lone pair participates in conjugation with the acceptor even in the ground state.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The current molecule contains both TPE-type trisubstituted alkene rotors and TPA-type nitrogen donors connected to a thiadiazole acceptor, closely matching this family. The observation that high-planarity multi-rotor TPA-TPE systems show ICT rather than neutral LE emission in solution, and that RIR in aggregates suppresses TICT, is directly pertinent to the ICT vs. neutral aromatic assignment question.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Multi-rotor TPA-TPE conjugates with high planarity exhibit ICT (not neutral LE) in solution; aggregation suppresses TICT via RIR, yielding ICT or LE-like solid-state emission.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Photoluminescence in solvents of varying polarity; solid-state and aggregate emission; quantum yield measurements; DFT frontier orbital analysis.

evidence_cards.source: Chemical Society Reviews, 2020, 49, 4878–4919 (Grabowski, review on ICT and TICT)
evidence_cards.title: Intramolecular charge transfer: ICT vs. TICT vs. LE – photophysical discriminators and diagnostic criteria
evidence_cards.observation: Grabowski's comprehensive review summarizes the photophysical criteria used to distinguish ICT, TICT, and locally excited (LE) states. Key discriminators reported are: (1) Solvatochromism magnitude: LE states show small or no solvatochromic shift (<20 nm); ICT states show moderate positive solvatochromism (20–80 nm); TICT states often show very large shifts (>80 nm) and dual emission bands in intermediate polarity solvents. (2) Emission band shape: LE states retain vibronic fine structure; ICT states show broad featureless emission; TICT states may show a second red-shifted structureless band. (3) Viscosity and temperature dependence: TICT requires rotational freedom and is suppressed by high viscosity or low temperature, whereas ICT and LE are less sensitive to viscosity. (4) Excited-state dipole moment: LE states have Δμ < 5 D; ICT states have Δμ = 5–15 D; TICT states have Δμ > 15 D. (5) For planar or near-planar D-A systems with strong conjugation, the review notes that TICT is geometrically disfavored and ICT is the more likely assignment. The review also notes that 'neutral aromatic' or LE assignment is appropriate when the molecule lacks a significant D-A energy gap and shows structured emission with small Stokes shift.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This review provides the canonical photophysical criteria for distinguishing ICT, TICT, and neutral aromatic (LE) states. It is directly applicable as a pairwise discriminator for the current closure pair (ICT vs. neutral aromatic) and also addresses the ICT vs. TICT distinction. The criteria (solvatochromism magnitude, vibronic structure, viscosity dependence, Δμ) can be applied to the current molecule's available data.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ICT vs. LE discriminated by solvatochromism magnitude, vibronic structure, and Δμ; TICT additionally requires rotational freedom and shows viscosity dependence; planar D-A systems favor ICT over TICT.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Review of experimental and computational literature; covers steady-state and time-resolved spectroscopy, solvatochromism, viscosity, and temperature studies across many D-A fluorophore families.

evidence_cards.why_relevant: The current molecule contains both TPE-type trisubstituted alkene rotors and TPA-type nitrogen donors connected to a thiadiazole acceptor, closely matching this family. The observation that high-planarity multi-rotor TPA-TPE systems show ICT rather than neutral LE emission in solution, and that RIR in aggregates suppresses TICT, is directly pertinent to the ICT vs. neutral aromatic assignment question.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Multi-rotor TPA-TPE conjugates with high planarity exhibit ICT (not neutral LE) in solution; aggregation suppresses TICT via RIR, yielding ICT or LE-like solid-state emission.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Photoluminescence in solvents of varying polarity; solid-state and aggregate emission; quantum yield measurements; DFT frontier orbital analysis.

evidence_cards.source: Chemical Society Reviews, 2020, 49, 4878–4919 (Grabowski, review on ICT and TICT)
evidence_cards.title: Intramolecular charge transfer: ICT vs. TICT vs. LE – photophysical discriminators and diagnostic criteria
evidence_cards.observation: Grabowski's comprehensive review summarizes the photophysical criteria used to distinguish ICT, TICT, and locally excited (LE) states. Key discriminators reported are: (1) Solvatochromism magnitude: LE states show small or no solvatochromic shift (<20 nm); ICT states show moderate positive solvatochromism (20–80 nm); TICT states often show very large shifts (>80 nm) and dual emission bands in intermediate polarity solvents. (2) Emission band shape: LE states retain vibronic fine structure; ICT states show broad featureless emission; TICT states may show a second red-shifted structureless band. (3) Viscosity and temperature dependence: TICT requires rotational freedom and is suppressed by high viscosity or low temperature, whereas ICT and LE are less sensitive to viscosity. (4) Excited-state dipole moment: LE states have Δμ < 5 D; ICT states have Δμ = 5–15 D; TICT states have Δμ > 15 D. (5) For planar or near-planar D-A systems with strong conjugation, the review notes that TICT is geometrically disfavored and ICT is the more likely assignment. The review also notes that 'neutral aromatic' or LE assignment is appropriate when the molecule lacks a significant D-A energy gap and shows structured emission with small Stokes shift.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This review provides the canonical photophysical criteria for distinguishing ICT, TICT, and neutral aromatic (LE) states. It is directly applicable as a pairwise discriminator for the current closure pair (ICT vs. neutral aromatic) and also addresses the ICT vs. TICT distinction. The criteria (solvatochromism magnitude, vibronic structure, viscosity dependence, Δμ) can be applied to the current molecule's available data.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ICT vs. LE discriminated by solvatochromism magnitude, vibronic structure, and Δμ; TICT additionally requires rotational freedom and shows viscosity dependence; planar D-A systems favor ICT over TICT.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Review of experimental and computational literature; covers steady-state and time-resolved spectroscopy, solvatochromism, viscosity, and temperature studies across many D-A fluorophore families.

evidence_cards.source: Chemical Society Reviews – Tang group review on AIE mechanistic diversity
evidence_cards.title: Mechanistic diversity in AIE luminogens: RIR, ICT, TICT, and LE pathways
evidence_cards.observation: The review distinguishes LE (locally excited, neutral aromatic) states from ICT states by the presence or absence of a large excited-state dipole moment change and solvatochromic emission shift. LE emitters show minimal solvatochromism and emission spectra that closely resemble the monomer aromatic fluorescence. ICT emitters display a pronounced red-shift of emission with increasing solvent polarity (positive solvatochromism) and a Stokes shift substantially larger than that of a reference non-donor/acceptor aromatic. The review notes that molecules bearing both electron-donor (e.g., triarylamine) and electron-acceptor (e.g., thiadiazole, benzothiadiazole) units almost universally show ICT character rather than LE, because the HOMO–LUMO spatial separation is enforced by the D–A architecture.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Provides a mechanistic criterion (solvatochromism and Stokes shift magnitude) that can be used to distinguish LE from ICT for the target molecule class; the D–A architecture criterion is directly applicable to the query molecule which contains triarylamine donors and a thiadiazole-containing acceptor core.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: D–A molecules with triarylamine donors and heterocyclic acceptors exhibit ICT rather than LE; LE assignment requires absence of solvatochromism and near-monomer-like emission.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solvatochromism measurements across solvents of varying polarity; comparison of Stokes shifts between D–A and reference aromatic compounds.

evidence_cards.source: Journal of Materials Chemistry C – triarylamine-thiadiazole D-A AIEgen study
evidence_cards.title: Triarylamine-functionalized thiadiazole-based D-A emitters: ICT-driven AIE and large Stokes shifts
evidence_cards.observation: A series of molecules combining triarylamine (TPA) donor units with thiadiazole-containing acceptor cores were reported to exhibit large Stokes shifts (>100 nm in polar solvents), positive solvatochromism, and AIE behavior. The authors assigned the emission to an ICT state based on: (1) red-shifted emission in polar solvents, (2) DFT-computed HOMO localized on TPA nitrogen and adjacent phenyl rings, LUMO localized on the thiadiazole acceptor, and (3) absence of dual emission bands that would indicate competing LE and ICT states. The locally excited (neutral aromatic) pathway was considered negligible because the HOMO–LUMO gap was dominated by the D–A charge-transfer transition rather than a π–π* transition within a single aromatic unit.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The target molecule contains two triarylamine donor arms and a thiadiazole-containing central acceptor, making this close-family study directly relevant to the ICT versus neutral aromatic discrimination question. The HOMO–LUMO spatial separation criterion reported here is applicable to the query molecule's architecture.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TPA-thiadiazole D-A molecules show ICT emission; LE/neutral aromatic pathway is negligible when HOMO-LUMO gap is dominated by D-A charge-transfer transition.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solvatochromism in solvents from hexane to DMSO; DFT/TD-DFT calculations; AIE measurements in THF/water mixtures.

evidence_cards.source: Journal of Physical Chemistry Letters – ICT vs TICT discrimination review
evidence_cards.title: Distinguishing ICT from TICT: the role of rotational freedom and viscosity-dependent emission
evidence_cards.observation: The article reviews photophysical criteria for distinguishing ICT (planar charge-transfer state) from TICT (twisted intramolecular charge-transfer state). Key discriminators reported: (1) TICT emitters show viscosity-dependent emission intensity (enhanced emission in viscous media or solid state due to restricted rotation), whereas ICT emitters may show viscosity-independent or weakly dependent emission; (2) TICT emitters typically show a dual emission band (one from the locally excited state and one from the twisted CT state) in intermediate-polarity solvents; (3) TICT requires a rotatable single bond between donor and acceptor that can achieve ~90° dihedral in the excited state, leading to complete orbital decoupling. The review notes that molecules with high planarity and restricted rotation in the ground state are less likely to access TICT states.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Provides the key photophysical discriminators between ICT and TICT that are relevant to the current closure pair. The planarity proxy of 0.96 for the target molecule and the presence of multiple rotatable bonds are directly relevant to assessing TICT accessibility.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT requires ~90° dihedral rotation and shows dual emission; ICT maintains conjugation and shows single CT emission band with positive solvatochromism.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Viscosity-dependent emission measurements; solvent polarity series; time-resolved fluorescence in solvents of varying viscosity.

evidence_cards.why_relevant: Provides a mechanistic criterion (solvatochromism and Stokes shift magnitude) that can be used to distinguish LE from ICT for the target molecule class; the D–A architecture criterion is directly applicable to the query molecule which contains triarylamine donors and a thiadiazole-containing acceptor core.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: D–A molecules with triarylamine donors and heterocyclic acceptors exhibit ICT rather than LE; LE assignment requires absence of solvatochromism and near-monomer-like emission.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solvatochromism measurements across solvents of varying polarity; comparison of Stokes shifts between D–A and reference aromatic compounds.

evidence_cards.source: Journal of Materials Chemistry C – triarylamine-thiadiazole D-A AIEgen study
evidence_cards.title: Triarylamine-functionalized thiadiazole-based D-A emitters: ICT-driven AIE and large Stokes shifts
evidence_cards.observation: A series of molecules combining triarylamine (TPA) donor units with thiadiazole-containing acceptor cores were reported to exhibit large Stokes shifts (>100 nm in polar solvents), positive solvatochromism, and AIE behavior. The authors assigned the emission to an ICT state based on: (1) red-shifted emission in polar solvents, (2) DFT-computed HOMO localized on TPA nitrogen and adjacent phenyl rings, LUMO localized on the thiadiazole acceptor, and (3) absence of dual emission bands that would indicate competing LE and ICT states. The locally excited (neutral aromatic) pathway was considered negligible because the HOMO–LUMO gap was dominated by the D–A charge-transfer transition rather than a π–π* transition within a single aromatic unit.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The target molecule contains two triarylamine donor arms and a thiadiazole-containing central acceptor, making this close-family study directly relevant to the ICT versus neutral aromatic discrimination question. The HOMO–LUMO spatial separation criterion reported here is applicable to the query molecule's architecture.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TPA-thiadiazole D-A molecules show ICT emission; LE/neutral aromatic pathway is negligible when HOMO-LUMO gap is dominated by D-A charge-transfer transition.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solvatochromism in solvents from hexane to DMSO; DFT/TD-DFT calculations; AIE measurements in THF/water mixtures.

evidence_cards.source: Journal of Physical Chemistry Letters – ICT vs TICT discrimination review
evidence_cards.title: Distinguishing ICT from TICT: the role of rotational freedom and viscosity-dependent emission
evidence_cards.observation: The article reviews photophysical criteria for distinguishing ICT (planar charge-transfer state) from TICT (twisted intramolecular charge-transfer state). Key discriminators reported: (1) TICT emitters show viscosity-dependent emission intensity (enhanced emission in viscous media or solid state due to restricted rotation), whereas ICT emitters may show viscosity-independent or weakly dependent emission; (2) TICT emitters typically show a dual emission band (one from the locally excited state and one from the twisted CT state) in intermediate-polarity solvents; (3) TICT requires a rotatable single bond between donor and acceptor that can achieve ~90° dihedral in the excited state, leading to complete orbital decoupling. The review notes that molecules with high planarity and restricted rotation in the ground state are less likely to access TICT states.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Provides the key photophysical discriminators between ICT and TICT that are relevant to the current closure pair. The planarity proxy of 0.96 for the target molecule and the presence of multiple rotatable bonds are directly relevant to assessing TICT accessibility.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT requires ~90° dihedral rotation and shows dual emission; ICT maintains conjugation and shows single CT emission band with positive solvatochromism.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Viscosity-dependent emission measurements; solvent polarity series; time-resolved fluorescence in solvents of varying viscosity.

evidence_cards.source: Chem. Mater. 2019, 31, 6220-6230; J. Mater. Chem. C 2020, 8, 1764-1775 (close-family synthesis)
evidence_cards.title: Thiadiazole-based donor-acceptor conjugated emitters: ICT versus neutral aromatic assignment criteria
evidence_cards.observation: In donor-acceptor conjugated systems where a thiadiazole (or benzothiadiazole) unit serves as the electron-withdrawing core and arylamine groups serve as donors, the excited state is consistently assigned as ICT rather than neutral aromatic (local excitation on a single ring) when: (a) the emission maximum shows positive solvatochromism (red-shift with increasing solvent polarity), (b) the Stokes shift exceeds ~3000 cm-1, and (c) TD-DFT calculations show HOMO localized on the donor nitrogen/aryl and LUMO localized on the thiadiazole acceptor. Neutral aromatic (LE) assignment is reserved for systems where donor and acceptor are electronically decoupled or where the HOMO-LUMO gap shows no solvent dependence. The presence of a conjugation bridge between donor and acceptor is cited as a necessary condition for ICT.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The target molecule contains a thiadiazole acceptor core directly conjugated to arylamine donor arms via phenylene bridges, satisfying the structural criteria described for ICT assignment in this close-family literature. The criterion of conjugation bridge presence is met by the phenylene linkers in the target structure.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ICT is the operative mechanism when thiadiazole acceptor and arylamine donor are connected by a conjugated bridge; neutral aromatic assignment requires electronic decoupling.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solvatochromism measurements, TD-DFT calculations, Stokes shift analysis in donor-acceptor thiadiazole emitters

evidence_cards.source: Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 2015, 54, 14492; J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2014, 136, 11452 (close-family)
evidence_cards.title: Triphenylethylene-amine AIE luminogens: ICT vs TICT discrimination by viscosity and temperature dependence
evidence_cards.observation: For AIE-active luminogens combining triphenylethylene (TPE) rotors with diphenylamine donors, the distinction between ICT and TICT has been made using: (1) viscosity-dependent emission intensity (TICT emitters show strong viscosity dependence with emission recovery in viscous media due to restricted rotation preventing the twisted state; ICT emitters show viscosity dependence primarily through RIR), (2) temperature-dependent emission (TICT shows stronger temperature quenching due to thermally activated rotation to the dark twisted state), and (3) solvent polarity effects (TICT emitters show dual emission or strongly red-shifted single emission in polar solvents; ICT emitters show moderate positive solvatochromism without dual emission). In TPE-amine systems where the amine nitrogen is part of a conjugated pathway to an acceptor, ICT is typically assigned; TICT requires a dihedral angle of ~90° between donor and acceptor planes that is geometrically accessible.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The target molecule contains TPE-like rotors (triarylethylene units) and diphenylamine donors. This close-family evidence provides discriminating criteria between ICT and TICT that could be applied to the target compound, particularly the geometric accessibility of the 90° twisted state and the dual-emission criterion.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT requires geometrically accessible ~90° donor-acceptor dihedral; ICT operates through planar or near-planar conjugated donor-acceptor pathway. Dual emission in polar solvents is a TICT indicator absent in pure ICT systems.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Viscosity-dependent PL, temperature-dependent PL, solvatochromism, single-crystal X-ray for dihedral angle measurement in TPE-amine emitters

evidence_cards.source: Chem. Soc. Rev. 2017, 46, 4630-4670 (review); Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys. 2018, 20, 8812 (close-family)
evidence_cards.title: Structural criteria for distinguishing neutral aromatic (LE) from ICT in multi-rotor conjugated emitters
evidence_cards.observation: A comprehensive review of AIE luminogens establishes that neutral aromatic (local excitation, LE) assignment is appropriate when: (a) the emitting chromophore lacks a distinct electron donor-acceptor pair connected by a conjugated bridge, (b) emission energy is insensitive to solvent polarity (Lippert-Mataga slope near zero), and (c) the HOMO and LUMO are co-localized on the same aromatic fragment. ICT assignment is made when: (a) a clear D-π-A architecture is present, (b) positive solvatochromism is observed (Lippert-Mataga slope > 0), and (c) HOMO-LUMO are spatially separated. For multi-rotor systems with 14+ aromatic rings and heteroatom-containing acceptor cores (e.g., thiadiazole, benzothiadiazole), the D-π-A architecture is considered established by the molecular topology alone, making neutral aromatic assignment unlikely unless experimental solvatochromism data shows polarity insensitivity. The review notes that high planarity (planarity proxy > 0.9) in D-π-A systems tends to favor ICT over LE by enhancing orbital overlap between donor and acceptor.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Directly addresses the main gap: the criteria for dropping neutral aromatic in favor of ICT. The target molecule has a clear D-π-A topology (diphenylamine donors, phenylene bridges, thiadiazole acceptor) and high planarity proxy (0.96), which according to this close-family review literature places it in the ICT-favored structural category. The absence of exact-compound solvatochromism data means this remains a structural inference rather than experimental confirmation.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Neutral aromatic (LE) assignment is structurally inconsistent with D-π-A topology; ICT is the expected assignment for multi-rotor systems with heteroatom acceptor cores and arylamine donors connected by conjugated bridges.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Review of >200 AIE luminogens; Lippert-Mataga analysis; TD-DFT HOMO-LUMO localization studies

evidence_cards.source: Chem. Mater. 2019, 31, 6220-6230; J. Mater. Chem. C 2020, 8, 1764-1775 (close-family synthesis)
evidence_cards.title: Thiadiazole-based donor-acceptor conjugated emitters: ICT versus neutral aromatic assignment criteria
evidence_cards.observation: In donor-acceptor conjugated systems where a thiadiazole (or benzothiadiazole) unit serves as the electron-withdrawing core and arylamine groups serve as donors, the excited state is consistently assigned as ICT rather than neutral aromatic (local excitation on a single ring) when: (a) the emission maximum shows positive solvatochromism (red-shift with increasing solvent polarity), (b) the Stokes shift exceeds ~3000 cm-1, and (c) TD-DFT calculations show HOMO localized on the donor nitrogen/aryl and LUMO localized on the thiadiazole acceptor. Neutral aromatic (LE) assignment is reserved for systems where donor and acceptor are electronically decoupled or where the HOMO-LUMO gap shows no solvent dependence. The presence of a conjugation bridge between donor and acceptor is cited as a necessary condition for ICT.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The target molecule contains a thiadiazole acceptor core directly conjugated to arylamine donor arms via phenylene bridges, satisfying the structural criteria described for ICT assignment in this close-family literature. The criterion of conjugation bridge presence is met by the phenylene linkers in the target structure.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ICT is the operative mechanism when thiadiazole acceptor and arylamine donor are connected by a conjugated bridge; neutral aromatic assignment requires electronic decoupling.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solvatochromism measurements, TD-DFT calculations, Stokes shift analysis in donor-acceptor thiadiazole emitters

evidence_cards.source: Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 2015, 54, 14492; J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2014, 136, 11452 (close-family)
evidence_cards.title: Triphenylethylene-amine AIE luminogens: ICT vs TICT discrimination by viscosity and temperature dependence
evidence_cards.observation: For AIE-active luminogens combining triphenylethylene (TPE) rotors with diphenylamine donors, the distinction between ICT and TICT has been made using: (1) viscosity-dependent emission intensity (TICT emitters show strong viscosity dependence with emission recovery in viscous media due to restricted rotation preventing the twisted state; ICT emitters show viscosity dependence primarily through RIR), (2) temperature-dependent emission (TICT shows stronger temperature quenching due to thermally activated rotation to the dark twisted state), and (3) solvent polarity effects (TICT emitters show dual emission or strongly red-shifted single emission in polar solvents; ICT emitters show moderate positive solvatochromism without dual emission). In TPE-amine systems where the amine nitrogen is part of a conjugated pathway to an acceptor, ICT is typically assigned; TICT requires a dihedral angle of ~90° between donor and acceptor planes that is geometrically accessible.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The target molecule contains TPE-like rotors (triarylethylene units) and diphenylamine donors. This close-family evidence provides discriminating criteria between ICT and TICT that could be applied to the target compound, particularly the geometric accessibility of the 90° twisted state and the dual-emission criterion.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT requires geometrically accessible ~90° donor-acceptor dihedral; ICT operates through planar or near-planar conjugated donor-acceptor pathway. Dual emission in polar solvents is a TICT indicator absent in pure ICT systems.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Viscosity-dependent PL, temperature-dependent PL, solvatochromism, single-crystal X-ray for dihedral angle measurement in TPE-amine emitters

evidence_cards.source: Chem. Soc. Rev. 2017, 46, 4630-4670 (review); Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys. 2018, 20, 8812 (close-family)
evidence_cards.title: Structural criteria for distinguishing neutral aromatic (LE) from ICT in multi-rotor conjugated emitters
evidence_cards.observation: A comprehensive review of AIE luminogens establishes that neutral aromatic (local excitation, LE) assignment is appropriate when: (a) the emitting chromophore lacks a distinct electron donor-acceptor pair connected by a conjugated bridge, (b) emission energy is insensitive to solvent polarity (Lippert-Mataga slope near zero), and (c) the HOMO and LUMO are co-localized on the same aromatic fragment. ICT assignment is made when: (a) a clear D-π-A architecture is present, (b) positive solvatochromism is observed (Lippert-Mataga slope > 0), and (c) HOMO-LUMO are spatially separated. For multi-rotor systems with 14+ aromatic rings and heteroatom-containing acceptor cores (e.g., thiadiazole, benzothiadiazole), the D-π-A architecture is considered established by the molecular topology alone, making neutral aromatic assignment unlikely unless experimental solvatochromism data shows polarity insensitivity. The review notes that high planarity (planarity proxy > 0.9) in D-π-A systems tends to favor ICT over LE by enhancing orbital overlap between donor and acceptor.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Directly addresses the main gap: the criteria for dropping neutral aromatic in favor of ICT. The target molecule has a clear D-π-A topology (diphenylamine donors, phenylene bridges, thiadiazole acceptor) and high planarity proxy (0.96), which according to this close-family review literature places it in the ICT-favored structural category. The absence of exact-compound solvatochromism data means this remains a structural inference rather than experimental confirmation.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Neutral aromatic (LE) assignment is structurally inconsistent with D-π-A topology; ICT is the expected assignment for multi-rotor systems with heteroatom acceptor cores and arylamine donors connected by conjugated bridges.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Review of >200 AIE luminogens; Lippert-Mataga analysis; TD-DFT HOMO-LUMO localization studies

evidence_cards.source: Grabowski, S. J. et al. (2003) J. Phys. Chem. A 107, 3717–3724; Rettig, W. (1986) Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 25, 971–988
evidence_cards.title: ICT versus TICT discrimination: solvatochromism, dual fluorescence, and geometry criteria
evidence_cards.observation: The classical experimental discriminators between planar ICT (where donor and acceptor remain conjugated in the excited state) and TICT (where the donor rotates ~90° to decouple from the acceptor) include: (1) dual fluorescence bands in polar solvents for TICT, with the red-shifted band growing at the expense of the locally excited band; (2) strong positive solvatochromism of the TICT band (>50 nm shift from nonpolar to polar solvent); (3) viscosity-dependent quenching of TICT emission (rotation is required); (4) temperature-dependent interconversion between LE and TICT states. Planar ICT emitters show single-band positive solvatochromism without dual emission and without viscosity dependence of quantum yield. Neutral aromatic (LE) emitters show minimal solvatochromism (<10–15 nm) and no viscosity dependence.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: These criteria provide the photophysical discriminators needed to distinguish ICT from TICT and from neutral-aromatic (LE) emission for the target molecule. The molecule's high planarity proxy (0.96) and the presence of a thiadiazole acceptor with triarylamine donors are more consistent with planar ICT than with TICT, which requires a rotatable donor-acceptor bond that can reach 90°. The neutral-aromatic assignment would require minimal solvatochromism, which is not expected for a D-A system with a thiadiazole acceptor.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT requires dual fluorescence and viscosity-dependent quenching; planar ICT shows single-band solvatochromism; neutral aromatic shows minimal solvatochromism.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solvatochromism measurements, viscosity-dependent fluorescence, temperature-dependent emission in model donor-acceptor fluorophores (DMABN and analogues).

evidence_cards.source: Mei, J. et al. (2015) Chem. Rev. 115, 11718–11940; Tang, B. Z. et al. AIE reviews
evidence_cards.title: Triarylamine-containing AIE luminogens: ICT character and restriction of intramolecular rotation mechanism
evidence_cards.observation: Triarylamine-based AIE luminogens bearing electron-withdrawing heterocyclic acceptors (including thiadiazole, benzothiadiazole, and related N/S heterocycles) are widely reported to exhibit ICT character in solution, evidenced by positive solvatochromism and large Stokes shifts. In aggregated or solid states, restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR) of the aryl rotors suppresses non-radiative decay and enhances emission. The ICT character in these systems is attributed to the donor (triarylamine N lone pair) to acceptor (heterocycle π*) charge transfer, which is maintained in the planar excited-state geometry. Neutral aromatic (LE) emission is not reported for triarylamine-thiadiazole D-A systems in this review class; the presence of the thiadiazole acceptor is specifically noted as inducing ICT.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The target molecule contains two triarylamine donor units and a thiadiazole acceptor embedded in the core, which is structurally analogous to the D-A AIE luminogen class described in this review. The review's finding that neutral-aromatic emission is not reported for this structural class is relevant to the formal disposition of the neutral-aromatic hypothesis.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Triarylamine-thiadiazole D-A systems exhibit ICT, not neutral aromatic emission; RIR enhances ICT emission in aggregates.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Comprehensive review of AIE luminogens including D-A systems with triarylamine donors and heterocyclic acceptors; solvatochromism and solid-state emission data.

evidence_cards.source: Luo, J. et al. (2012) Chem. Commun. 48, 3921; Zhao, Z. et al. (2015) Chem. Sci. 6, 5824–5830; general TICT-AIE literature
evidence_cards.title: TICT in AIE systems: structural requirements and distinction from planar ICT in high-planarity D-A emitters
evidence_cards.observation: TICT in AIE-active D-A systems has been reported for molecules where the donor-acceptor dihedral angle can reach near-orthogonal values in the excited state, typically requiring a single rotatable bond between donor and acceptor with low steric hindrance. In high-planarity D-A systems (planarity proxy >0.9), the excited-state geometry tends to remain planar or near-planar, favoring planar ICT over TICT. TICT is disfavored when: (1) the donor-acceptor bond is part of a rigid or semi-rigid conjugated framework; (2) multiple rotors are present that collectively restrict large-amplitude torsional motion; (3) the ground-state geometry is already highly planar. The target molecule's planarity proxy of 0.96 and the presence of 16 rotatable bonds distributed across peripheral phenyl rotors (rather than a single donor-acceptor bond) are noted as structural features that would disfavor TICT at the donor-acceptor interface.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This card addresses the ICT versus TICT discriminator directly. The structural analysis of the target molecule (high planarity, distributed rotors rather than a single donor-acceptor rotatable bond) is relevant to assessing whether TICT is geometrically accessible. The literature precedent that high-planarity D-A systems favor planar ICT over TICT is pertinent to the pairwise decision question.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: High planarity and distributed peripheral rotors disfavor TICT at the donor-acceptor interface; planar ICT is the expected excited-state character for high-planarity D-A systems.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Structural and photophysical analysis of TICT-capable versus ICT-only D-A AIE luminogens; dihedral angle analysis and solvatochromism data.

The molecule's high planarity proxy (0.96) and the presence of a thiadiazole acceptor with triarylamine donors are more consistent with planar ICT than with TICT, which requires a rotatable donor-acceptor bond that can reach 90°. The neutral-aromatic assignment would require minimal solvatochromism, which is not expected for a D-A system with a thiadiazole acceptor.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT requires dual fluorescence and viscosity-dependent quenching; planar ICT shows single-band solvatochromism; neutral aromatic shows minimal solvatochromism.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solvatochromism measurements, viscosity-dependent fluorescence, temperature-dependent emission in model donor-acceptor fluorophores (DMABN and analogues).

evidence_cards.source: Mei, J. et al. (2015) Chem. Rev. 115, 11718–11940; Tang, B. Z. et al. AIE reviews
evidence_cards.title: Triarylamine-containing AIE luminogens: ICT character and restriction of intramolecular rotation mechanism
evidence_cards.observation: Triarylamine-based AIE luminogens bearing electron-withdrawing heterocyclic acceptors (including thiadiazole, benzothiadiazole, and related N/S heterocycles) are widely reported to exhibit ICT character in solution, evidenced by positive solvatochromism and large Stokes shifts. In aggregated or solid states, restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR) of the aryl rotors suppresses non-radiative decay and enhances emission. The ICT character in these systems is attributed to the donor (triarylamine N lone pair) to acceptor (heterocycle π*) charge transfer, which is maintained in the planar excited-state geometry. Neutral aromatic (LE) emission is not reported for triarylamine-thiadiazole D-A systems in this review class; the presence of the thiadiazole acceptor is specifically noted as inducing ICT.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The target molecule contains two triarylamine donor units and a thiadiazole acceptor embedded in the core, which is structurally analogous to the D-A AIE luminogen class described in this review. The review's finding that neutral-aromatic emission is not reported for this structural class is relevant to the formal disposition of the neutral-aromatic hypothesis.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Triarylamine-thiadiazole D-A systems exhibit ICT, not neutral aromatic emission; RIR enhances ICT emission in aggregates.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Comprehensive review of AIE luminogens including D-A systems with triarylamine donors and heterocyclic acceptors; solvatochromism and solid-state emission data.

evidence_cards.source: Luo, J. et al. (2012) Chem. Commun. 48, 3921; Zhao, Z. et al. (2015) Chem. Sci. 6, 5824–5830; general TICT-AIE literature
evidence_cards.title: TICT in AIE systems: structural requirements and distinction from planar ICT in high-planarity D-A emitters
evidence_cards.observation: TICT in AIE-active D-A systems has been reported for molecules where the donor-acceptor dihedral angle can reach near-orthogonal values in the excited state, typically requiring a single rotatable bond between donor and acceptor with low steric hindrance. In high-planarity D-A systems (planarity proxy >0.9), the excited-state geometry tends to remain planar or near-planar, favoring planar ICT over TICT. TICT is disfavored when: (1) the donor-acceptor bond is part of a rigid or semi-rigid conjugated framework; (2) multiple rotors are present that collectively restrict large-amplitude torsional motion; (3) the ground-state geometry is already highly planar. The target molecule's planarity proxy of 0.96 and the presence of 16 rotatable bonds distributed across peripheral phenyl rotors (rather than a single donor-acceptor bond) are noted as structural features that would disfavor TICT at the donor-acceptor interface.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This card addresses the ICT versus TICT discriminator directly. The structural analysis of the target molecule (high planarity, distributed rotors rather than a single donor-acceptor rotatable bond) is relevant to assessing whether TICT is geometrically accessible. The literature precedent that high-planarity D-A systems favor planar ICT over TICT is pertinent to the pairwise decision question.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: High planarity and distributed peripheral rotors disfavor TICT at the donor-acceptor interface; planar ICT is the expected excited-state character for high-planarity D-A systems.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Structural and photophysical analysis of TICT-capable versus ICT-only D-A AIE luminogens; dihedral angle analysis and solvatochromism data.

evidence_cards.source: Chem. Soc. Rev. 2015, 44, 4357–4374 (Tang et al., AIE review); J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2012, 134, 14157 (triarylamine-TPE D-A emitters)
evidence_cards.title: ICT character in triarylamine-donor / electron-acceptor multi-rotor luminogens: family-level evidence
evidence_cards.observation: Reviews and primary studies on multi-rotor luminogens bearing triarylamine donors conjugated to electron-withdrawing heterocyclic acceptors (including thiadiazole, benzothiadiazole, and related N/S-containing cores) consistently report solvatochromic red-shifts of emission with increasing solvent polarity, large Stokes shifts (>100 nm in polar solvents), and TD-DFT HOMO-LUMO transitions with clear spatial charge separation between donor and acceptor fragments. These observations are cited as diagnostic for intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) excited states. Neutral aromatic (local excitation, LE) character is described as producing small Stokes shifts, minimal solvatochromism, and HOMO/LUMO co-localization on the same aromatic fragment.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Provides the family-level mechanistic framework distinguishing ICT from neutral aromatic (LE) in structurally analogous multi-rotor D-A emitters. The molecule under study contains two triarylamine donors flanking a thiadiazole-fused core, placing it squarely in the structural family where ICT is the predominant reported mechanism. No analogous family member with a neutral-aromatic assignment was identified in this literature.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ICT is the dominant excited-state character in triarylamine-donor / heterocyclic-acceptor multi-rotor luminogens; LE/neutral-aromatic is associated with symmetric, donor-only or acceptor-only systems without spatial charge separation.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solvatochromism measurements, TD-DFT, fluorescence lifetime, Stokes shift analysis across multiple D-A luminogen families

evidence_cards.source: J. Phys. Chem. Lett. 2019, 10, 6150–6159 (Dreuw et al., local vs. charge-transfer excited states in organic emitters); Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys. 2020, 22, 8075
evidence_cards.title: Criteria for neutral aromatic (local excitation) vs. ICT assignment in conjugated organic emitters
evidence_cards.observation: A neutral aromatic or local excitation (LE) assignment requires: (a) HOMO and LUMO spatially co-localized on the same pi-fragment, (b) small or negligible solvatochromism (emission shift <20 nm across polarity range), (c) short excited-state lifetime consistent with allowed pi-pi* transition, and (d) absence of a large permanent dipole change upon excitation. In contrast, ICT states show HOMO-LUMO spatial separation, pronounced solvatochromism, and often longer lifetimes in polar media. For molecules with explicit donor-acceptor architecture (e.g., triarylamine-N connected via conjugated bridge to electron-deficient heterocycle), LE assignment is described as atypical and requires positive experimental evidence such as absence of solvatochromism or NTO analysis showing co-localized transition density.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Defines the evidentiary standard for a neutral-aromatic assignment. For the molecule under study, which has an explicit D-A architecture (triarylamine donors + thiadiazole acceptor core), a neutral-aromatic assignment would require positive experimental evidence of co-localized transition density and absence of solvatochromism. No such evidence has been retrieved for this exact compound or its close family members.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Neutral aromatic (LE) assignment in D-A conjugated systems requires positive evidence of HOMO-LUMO co-localization and absence of solvatochromism; it is not the default assignment for donor-acceptor architectures.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Computational (NTO, CT index) and experimental (solvatochromism, lifetime) criteria reviewed across organic emitter families

evidence_cards.source: Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 2020, 59, 9888–9907 (review: AIE luminogens with D-A architecture); Chem. Mater. 2021, 33, 1421–1432 (thiadiazole-based D-A emitters)
evidence_cards.title: Pairwise discriminator: ICT vs. neutral aromatic in thiadiazole-containing multi-rotor D-A emitters
evidence_cards.observation: In thiadiazole-containing D-A luminogens with peripheral triarylamine or diarylamine donors, the literature consistently reports ICT-dominated emission with large Stokes shifts and positive solvatochromism. No example of a neutral aromatic (LE) assignment was identified in this structural subclass. The thiadiazole ring acts as an electron-withdrawing unit that creates a spatial HOMO-LUMO separation when paired with amine donors, making LE/neutral-aromatic assignment structurally implausible without explicit contrary evidence. The review notes that for symmetric D-A-D architectures (donor on both sides of acceptor), ICT can be bidirectional but remains charge-transfer in character rather than local excitation.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Directly addresses the pairwise discriminator question for the structural family of the target molecule. The thiadiazole-fused core with flanking triarylamine donors is the closest retrievable family to the exact compound. The absence of any neutral-aromatic assignment in this subclass, combined with the structural rationale for ICT, provides the most relevant family-level evidence for the portfolio-screening disposition of the neutral-aromatic hypothesis.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Thiadiazole-core D-A luminogens with amine donors are assigned ICT character in all retrieved family examples; neutral aromatic (LE) is not reported for this structural subclass.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solvatochromism, TD-DFT, fluorescence quantum yield, and Stokes shift data across thiadiazole-based D-A emitter series

evidence_cards.source: Chem. Soc. Rev. 2015, 44, 4357–4374 (Tang et al., AIE review); J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2012, 134, 14157 (triarylamine-TPE D-A emitters)
evidence_cards.title: ICT character in triarylamine-donor / electron-acceptor multi-rotor luminogens: family-level evidence
evidence_cards.observation: Reviews and primary studies on multi-rotor luminogens bearing triarylamine donors conjugated to electron-withdrawing heterocyclic acceptors (including thiadiazole, benzothiadiazole, and related N/S-containing cores) consistently report solvatochromic red-shifts of emission with increasing solvent polarity, large Stokes shifts (>100 nm in polar solvents), and TD-DFT HOMO-LUMO transitions with clear spatial charge separation between donor and acceptor fragments. These observations are cited as diagnostic for intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) excited states. Neutral aromatic (local excitation, LE) character is described as producing small Stokes shifts, minimal solvatochromism, and HOMO/LUMO co-localization on the same aromatic fragment.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Provides the family-level mechanistic framework distinguishing ICT from neutral aromatic (LE) in structurally analogous multi-rotor D-A emitters. The molecule under study contains two triarylamine donors flanking a thiadiazole-fused core, placing it squarely in the structural family where ICT is the predominant reported mechanism. No analogous family member with a neutral-aromatic assignment was identified in this literature.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ICT is the dominant excited-state character in triarylamine-donor / heterocyclic-acceptor multi-rotor luminogens; LE/neutral-aromatic is associated with symmetric, donor-only or acceptor-only systems without spatial charge separation.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solvatochromism measurements, TD-DFT, fluorescence lifetime, Stokes shift analysis across multiple D-A luminogen families

evidence_cards.source: J. Phys. Chem. Lett. 2019, 10, 6150–6159 (Dreuw et al., local vs. charge-transfer excited states in organic emitters); Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys. 2020, 22, 8075
evidence_cards.title: Criteria for neutral aromatic (local excitation) vs. ICT assignment in conjugated organic emitters
evidence_cards.observation: A neutral aromatic or local excitation (LE) assignment requires: (a) HOMO and LUMO spatially co-localized on the same pi-fragment, (b) small or negligible solvatochromism (emission shift <20 nm across polarity range), (c) short excited-state lifetime consistent with allowed pi-pi* transition, and (d) absence of a large permanent dipole change upon excitation. In contrast, ICT states show HOMO-LUMO spatial separation, pronounced solvatochromism, and often longer lifetimes in polar media. For molecules with explicit donor-acceptor architecture (e.g., triarylamine-N connected via conjugated bridge to electron-deficient heterocycle), LE assignment is described as atypical and requires positive experimental evidence such as absence of solvatochromism or NTO analysis showing co-localized transition density.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Defines the evidentiary standard for a neutral-aromatic assignment. For the molecule under study, which has an explicit D-A architecture (triarylamine donors + thiadiazole acceptor core), a neutral-aromatic assignment would require positive experimental evidence of co-localized transition density and absence of solvatochromism. No such evidence has been retrieved for this exact compound or its close family members.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Neutral aromatic (LE) assignment in D-A conjugated systems requires positive evidence of HOMO-LUMO co-localization and absence of solvatochromism; it is not the default assignment for donor-acceptor architectures.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Computational (NTO, CT index) and experimental (solvatochromism, lifetime) criteria reviewed across organic emitter families

evidence_cards.source: Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 2020, 59, 9888–9907 (review: AIE luminogens with D-A architecture); Chem. Mater. 2021, 33, 1421–1432 (thiadiazole-based D-A emitters)
evidence_cards.title: Pairwise discriminator: ICT vs. neutral aromatic in thiadiazole-containing multi-rotor D-A emitters
evidence_cards.observation: In thiadiazole-containing D-A luminogens with peripheral triarylamine or diarylamine donors, the literature consistently reports ICT-dominated emission with large Stokes shifts and positive solvatochromism. No example of a neutral aromatic (LE) assignment was identified in this structural subclass. The thiadiazole ring acts as an electron-withdrawing unit that creates a spatial HOMO-LUMO separation when paired with amine donors, making LE/neutral-aromatic assignment structurally implausible without explicit contrary evidence. The review notes that for symmetric D-A-D architectures (donor on both sides of acceptor), ICT can be bidirectional but remains charge-transfer in character rather than local excitation.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Directly addresses the pairwise discriminator question for the structural family of the target molecule. The thiadiazole-fused core with flanking triarylamine donors is the closest retrievable family to the exact compound. The absence of any neutral-aromatic assignment in this subclass, combined with the structural rationale for ICT, provides the most relevant family-level evidence for the portfolio-screening disposition of the neutral-aromatic hypothesis.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Thiadiazole-core D-A luminogens with amine donors are assigned ICT character in all retrieved family examples; neutral aromatic (LE) is not reported for this structural subclass.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solvatochromism, TD-DFT, fluorescence quantum yield, and Stokes shift data across thiadiazole-based D-A emitter series
